criteria: The agent has read Section 1 (Introduction) completely to understand the motivation for using Mamba-based state space modeling over CNN and Transformer architectures for 3D medical image segmentation.
The agent has read Section 2 and Figure 1 completely to understand the three main components of SegMamba: the TSMamba-block encoder, the convolutional decoder, and the FUE-enhanced skip connections.

The plan includes the overall U-shaped encoder-decoder architecture with a stem layer, four stages of TSMamba blocks in the encoder, a convolutional decoder, and feature-level uncertainty estimation (FUE) on the skip connections.
The plan includes the stem layer design as a depth-wise convolution with kernel size 7x7x7, padding 3x3x3, and stride 2x2x2.

The code for the stem layer has been implemented as a depth-wise convolution with kernel 7x7x7, padding 3x3x3, and stride 2x2x2, producing the first-scale feature z0 with 48 channels at half the input spatial resolution.
The agent has read Section 2.1 and Equation 1 completely to understand the computation flow of the Tri-orientated Spatial Mamba (TSMamba) block.

The plan includes the TSMamba block design following Equation 1: GSC applied to the input, followed by LayerNorm and ToM with a residual connection, then LayerNorm and an MLP with a second residual connection.
The code for the TSMamba block forward pass has been implemented exactly as Equation 1: GSC(z) first, then ToM(LN(.))  added back as a residual, then MLP(LN(.)) added back as a second residual.
The code implements the TSMamba block as a repeatable unit stacked Nm times within each of the four encoder stages, with down-sampling layers placed between consecutive stages.

The plan includes the gated spatial convolution (GSC) module design with two parallel convolution branches whose outputs are multiplied element-wise as a gating mechanism before a final fusing convolution and residual connection.

The code for the GSC module has been implemented as Equation 2: two convolution blocks (kernel 3x3x3 and kernel 1x1x1, each containing a norm, convolution, and nonlinearity) whose outputs are multiplied pixel-by-pixel, passed through a fusing 3x3x3 convolution block, and added back to the original input via a residual connection.

The plan includes the tri-orientated Mamba (ToM) module design, flattening the 3D feature map into three sequences (forward, reverse, inter-slice) and applying a Mamba layer to each before summing the outputs.

The code for the ToM module has been implemented as Equation 3: the 3D input feature is flattened into three separate sequences (forward, reverse, inter-slice), each independently processed by a Mamba layer, and the three resulting outputs are summed to form the fused output.

A Mamba layer (selective state space model, e.g. via the mamba-ssm library or an equivalent custom selective SSM implementation) is integrated as the core sequence-modeling operator used inside the ToM module.
The reverse-direction sequence used in ToM is implemented as the flipped/reversed ordering of the forward-flattened sequence and passed through its own Mamba layer instance.

The inter-slice-direction sequence used in ToM is implemented as an alternate axis-flattening order (e.g. transposing the slice/depth axis to be outermost) that is distinct from the forward and reverse flattenings.
The agent has read Section 2.2 and Equation 4 completely to understand the feature-level uncertainty estimation (FUE) module used at the skip connections.

The plan includes the FUE module design: computing a per-scale uncertainty map from the channel-wise mean feature via a sigmoid and entropy formula, then using it to reweight the corresponding skip-connection feature.

The code for the FUE module's uncertainty estimation has been implemented as Equation 4: the channel-wise mean of the scale-i feature is computed, normalized with a sigmoid to obtain z-bar, and the uncertainty u^i is computed as -z-bar * log(z-bar).
The code implements the final FUE-enhanced skip-connection feature as z-tilde = z + z * (1 - u), applied independently at each encoder scale before it is fused into the decoder.
The plan includes the encoder architecture progressively down-sampling through four TSMamba-block stages, increasing channel width up to 768 channels at the deepest scale (D/32 x H/32 x W/32).

The code for the encoder has been implemented as the stem layer followed by four sequential stages of TSMamba blocks separated by down-sampling layers, with channel width progressively increasing to 768 at the final (D/32 x H/32 x W/32) scale.
The plan includes the decoder design using transpose-convolution upsampling and residual blocks at each scale, fusing in the FUE-processed encoder skip features.

The code for the decoder has been implemented with transpose-convolution upsampling and residual blocks at each scale, progressively fusing the FUE-processed skip-connection features from the corresponding encoder stage.
The code for the segmentation head has been implemented to project the final decoder feature map to per-voxel class logits matching the original input spatial resolution.
The agent has read Section 3.1 and Table 1 completely to understand the construction and statistics of the proposed CRC-500 dataset.
The agent has read Section 3.2 to understand the BraTS2023 and AIIB2023 public benchmark datasets used for evaluation.
The plan includes handling of the CRC-500 dataset (500 3D CT volumes with expert annotation and calibration by a second professional doctor).
The plan includes loading the BraTS2023 dataset with its four MRI modalities (T1, T1Gd, T2, T2-FLAIR) and three segmentation targets (WT, TC, ET).
The plan includes loading the AIIB2023 dataset (120 high-resolution CT scans) for airway-tree segmentation.

The code for loading and preprocessing the CRC-500 dataset has been implemented, correctly handling the 512x512 in-plane CT volumes with variable z-axis depth (94-238 slices) and the reported voxel spacing ranges.
The code for loading and preprocessing the BraTS2023 dataset has been implemented, correctly stacking the four MRI modalities as input channels and constructing the WT/TC/ET segmentation label maps.
The code for loading and preprocessing the AIIB2023 dataset has been implemented for airway-tree segmentation from CT scans.
The code implements a dataset split that randomly allocates 70% of volumes for training, 10% for validation, and 20% for testing, applied independently to each of the three datasets.
The agent has read the 'Implementation Details' paragraph in Section 3.2 completely to extract the full training configuration used across all experiments.
The plan includes the software framework versions used for the implementation: PyTorch 2.0.1-cuda11.7 and MONAI 1.2.0.
The plan includes a random crop size of 128x128x128 and a batch size of 2 per GPU for training on each dataset.
The plan includes cross-entropy loss as the training objective across all experiments.
The plan includes an SGD optimizer with a polynomial learning-rate scheduler (initial learning rate of 1e-2, decay of 1e-5).
The plan includes training for 1000 epochs on all datasets.
The plan includes the data augmentation pipeline: additive brightness, gamma adjustment, rotation, scaling, mirroring, and elastic deformation.
The code for the training pipeline has been implemented using a random crop size of 128x128x128 with batch size 2 per GPU as the input sampling strategy.
The cross-entropy loss function has been implemented/configured as the training objective for all segmentation experiments.
The SGD optimizer with a polynomial-decay learning rate scheduler has been implemented with an initial learning rate of 1e-2 and decay of 1e-5.
The data augmentation pipeline has been implemented including additive brightness, gamma correction, rotation, scaling, mirroring, and elastic deformation transforms.
The training loop has been configured to run for 1000 epochs on each dataset.
Multi-GPU training has been implemented/configured to leverage the four NVIDIA A100 GPUs described for the experiments.
The agent has read Section 3.3 completely to identify the six state-of-the-art baseline methods compared against SegMamba.
The plan includes implementing/integrating the three CNN-based baselines: SegresNet, UX-Net (3D UX-Net), and MedNeXt.
The plan includes implementing/integrating the three Transformer-based baselines: UNETR, SwinUNETR, and SwinUNETR-V2.
The plan includes retraining all six baseline methods using their public implementations under the same data splits and training settings as SegMamba for a fair comparison.
The SegresNet baseline has been integrated and retrained using its public implementation under the same training configuration as SegMamba.
The UX-Net baseline has been integrated and retrained using its public implementation under the same training configuration as SegMamba.
The MedNeXt baseline has been integrated and retrained using its public implementation under the same training configuration as SegMamba.
The UNETR baseline has been integrated and retrained using its public implementation under the same training configuration as SegMamba.
The SwinUNETR baseline has been integrated and retrained using its public implementation under the same training configuration as SegMamba.
The SwinUNETR-V2 baseline has been integrated and retrained using its public implementation under the same training configuration as SegMamba.
The plan includes the evaluation metrics for BraTS2023 and CRC-500: Dice score and 95% Hausdorff Distance (HD95).
The plan includes the AIIB2023-specific evaluation metrics: Intersection over Union (IoU), Detected Length Ratio (DLR), and Detected Branch Ratio (DBR).
The Dice score metric has been implemented for evaluation on the BraTS2023 and CRC-500 datasets.
The 95% Hausdorff Distance (HD95) metric has been implemented for evaluation on the BraTS2023 and CRC-500 datasets.
The IoU, Detected Length Ratio (DLR), and Detected Branch Ratio (DBR) metrics have been implemented for evaluation on the AIIB2023 dataset, following the protocol of the original AIIB2023 challenge.
SegMamba training has been executed successfully without errors on the BraTS2023 dataset.
SegMamba training has been executed successfully without errors on the AIIB2023 dataset.
SegMamba training has been executed successfully without errors on the CRC-500 dataset.
All six baseline methods have been trained successfully without errors under the same settings on all three datasets.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: SegMamba achieves the best average Dice (91.32%) and lowest average HD95 (3.56) across WT/TC/ET on BraTS2023, outperforming all six baselines.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: SegMamba achieves the highest IoU (88.59%), DLR (70.21%), and DBR (61.33%) on AIIB2023, outperforming all six baselines.
The execution outcomes match Table 3: SegMamba achieves the best Dice (48.46%) and HD95 (28.52) on the CRC-500 dataset, outperforming all six baselines.

The execution outcomes match Figure 4: SegMamba's qualitative segmentation boundaries/regions are visually closer to the ground truth than the baseline methods across the BraTS2023, CRC-500, and AIIB2023 datasets.
The agent has read Section 3.4 and Table 4 completely to understand the module ablation study examining the contributions of GSC, ToM, and FUE.

The plan includes the module ablation study on CRC-500 with variants M1 (baseline using only the original single-direction Mamba layer), M2 (+GSC), M3 (+ToM), M4 (+GSC+ToM), and the full model (+GSC+ToM+FUE).
The code implements ablation variant M1, the basic method containing only the original single-direction Mamba layer with no GSC, ToM, or FUE modules.
The code implements ablation variant M2, adding only the GSC module on top of the M1 baseline.
The code implements ablation variant M3, adding only the ToM module (without GSC) on top of the M1 baseline.
The code implements ablation variant M4, combining both the GSC and ToM modules without the FUE module.
All module ablation variants (M1, M2, M3, M4, and the full SegMamba model) have been executed successfully without errors on the CRC-500 dataset.
The execution outcomes match Table 4: progressively adding GSC, then ToM, then FUE yields a monotonic improvement in Dice and reduction in HD95 over the M1 baseline on CRC-500.
The agent has read Table 5 completely to understand the efficiency ablation comparing core global-modeling modules (large-kernel convolution, SwinTransformer, self-attention, and TSMamba).

The plan includes the efficiency ablation that replaces the TSMamba core module with large-kernel convolution, SwinTransformer, and self-attention, evaluated at 128^3 input resolution with a flattened sequence length of 262144.

The code implements the efficiency ablation variants by substituting the TSMamba module's core global-modeling operator with large-kernel convolution and with a SwinTransformer block, keeping the rest of the architecture fixed.
The code implements a self-attention variant of the core module for the efficiency ablation, used to demonstrate its infeasibility at full 3D resolution.
Profiling code has been implemented to measure training memory (TM), inference memory (IM), and inference time (IT) at 128^3 input resolution for each core-module variant.
The efficiency ablation variants have been executed at 128^3 resolution, correctly reproducing the out-of-memory (OOM) failure for the self-attention variant.

The execution outcomes match Table 5: TSMamba achieves lower training memory and lower inference time than the large-kernel-convolution and SwinTransformer variants while retaining a global receptive field, unlike the self-attention variant which goes out of memory.
